file.content:
# Review: deepmind/46.lean

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

No issues.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports` at line 17.
- [x] No other imports are present.

No issues.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line (lines 19–28).
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 46`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/46`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem.
- [x] Bibliography entry for Croot [Cr03] is included.

No issues.

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos46 ... end Erdos46`.
- [x] Namespace name uses UpperCamelCase (`Erdos46`).
- [x] `open Finset BigOperators` appears **before** the namespace block (line 30).

No issues.

## 5. Custom Definitions

N/A — no custom `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations in this file.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem named `erdos_46` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
- [x] Name follows Lean/Mathlib conventions (snake_case).
- [x] Has `@[category ..., AMS ...]` on the same attribute line (line 41).
- [x] Has a docstring (lines 34–39).
- [x] Docstring references the source paper [Cr03].

### `answer()` usage

- [ ] **FAIL — wrong answer() case pattern.**

  The **original problem** on [erdosproblems.com/46](https://www.erdosproblems.com/46) is phrased as a yes/no question:

  > "Does every finite colouring of the integers have a monochromatic solution to $1 = \sum 1/n_i$ with $2 \le n_1 < \cdots < n_k$?"

  Per the checklist (§6, "How to decide which case applies"), a "Does..." question is **Case 1** and should use the `answer() ↔ P` pattern. Since it has been solved affirmatively by Croot, it should be:

  ```lean
  theorem erdos_46 : answer(True) ↔
      ∀ (α : Type*) [Finite α] (c : ℕ → α),
        ∃ S : Finset ℕ, S.Nonempty ∧
          (∀ n ∈ S, n ≥ 2) ∧
          (∃ color : α, ∀ n ∈ S, c n = color) ∧
          (∑ n ∈ S, (1 : ℚ) / (n : ℚ)) = 1 := by sorry
  ```

  Currently the theorem uses a direct assertion (Case 3 pattern) with quantifiers as theorem parameters. Two sub-issues:

  1. Missing `answer(True) ↔ ...` wrapper (required for yes/no questions).
  2. Quantifiers `(α : Type*) [Finite α] (c : ℕ → α)` are theorem parameters instead of appearing after `answer()` on the RHS. The checklist common rule states: "Quantifiers go **after** `answer(sorry)`, not as theorem parameters."

  The module docstring rephrases the problem as a direct assertion, but the checklist explicitly says: *"The rule depends on how the **original problem is phrased in English**, not how the Lean code currently looks."*

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[category ...]` attribute.
- [x] Value is `research solved` — appropriate since Croot proved it but the proof is not formally verified here.
- N/A — not `research open`, so no sorry-free constraint applies.

No issues.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute: `AMS 5 11`.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed (two: 5, 11).
- [x] Numbers in ascending order: 5 < 11.
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] Both 5 (Combinatorics) and 11 (Number theory) are valid AMS MSC2020 codes.

No issues.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Uses multi-line `by\n  sorry` form (lines 46–47), which is acceptable.
- [x] No `sorry` in def/abbrev/structure (N/A — none exist).
- [x] No informal proof sketches.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

No issues.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

N/A — no variants in this file.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used (`∃`, `∈`, `≥`, `∑`, `ℚ`).
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [ ] **MINOR — one long line.** Line 27 (bibliography entry) is 117 characters, exceeding the ~100-character guideline. This is in a docstring comment and is a citation, so it is difficult to break cleanly. Low priority.
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] Not verified (requires `lake build`). No obvious issues expected.

---

## Summary

| Section | Status |
|---|---|
| 1. Copyright Header | PASS |
| 2. Import Statement | PASS |
| 3. Module Docstring | PASS |
| 4. Namespace | PASS |
| 5. Custom Definitions | N/A |
| 6. Theorem Statements / `answer()` | **FAIL** |
| 7. Category Attribute | PASS |
| 8. AMS Attribute | PASS |
| 9. Proof Bodies | PASS |
| 10. Variants | N/A |
| 11. Code Quality | MINOR |
| 12. Build Verification | NOT VERIFIED |

### Required changes

1. **answer() pattern (§6):** The original problem is a yes/no question ("Does every finite colouring..."). Rewrite as `answer(True) ↔ P` with all quantifiers on the RHS, not as theorem parameters.

### Suggested changes

2. **Line length (§11):** Consider breaking the bibliography line 27 (117 chars) if possible, though citation lines in docstrings are low priority.

file.content:
/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erdős Problem 46

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/46](https://www.erdosproblems.com/46)

For every finite colouring of the integers $\geq 2$, there exists a monochromatic finite
set whose reciprocals sum to $1$. Proved by Croot [Cr03].

[Cr03] Croot, E.S., _On a coloring conjecture about unit fractions_, Annals of Mathematics **157** (2003), 545–556.
-/

open Finset BigOperators

namespace Erdos46

/--
For every finite colouring of the positive integers $\ge 2$, there exists a
monochromatic finite set $\{n_1, \ldots, n_k\}$ with $2 \le n_1 < \cdots < n_k$ whose
reciprocals sum to $1$, i.e. $\sum 1/n_i = 1$.

Proved by Croot [Cr03].
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
theorem erdos_46 (α : Type*) [Finite α] (c : ℕ → α) :
    ∃ S : Finset ℕ, S.Nonempty ∧
      (∀ n ∈ S, n ≥ 2) ∧
      (∃ color : α, ∀ n ∈ S, c n = color) ∧
      (∑ n ∈ S, (1 : ℚ) / (n : ℚ)) = 1 := by
  sorry

end Erdos46

content.text: The fix is clear: wrap the statement in `answer(True) ↔ ...` and move the quantifiers from theorem parameters to the RHS.

  sorry

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erdős Problem 46

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/46](https://www.erdosproblems.com/46)

For every finite colouring of the integers $\geq 2$, there exists a monochromatic finite
set whose reciprocals sum to $1$. Proved by Croot [Cr03].

[Cr03] Croot, E.S., _On a coloring conjecture about unit fractions_, Annals of Mathematics **157** (2003), 545–556.
-/

open Finset BigOperators

namespace Erdos46

/--
For every finite colouring of the positive integers $\ge 2$, there exists a
monochromatic finite set $\{n_1, \ldots, n_k\}$ with $2 \le n_1 < \cdots < n_k$ whose
reciprocals sum to $1$, i.e. $\sum 1/n_i = 1$.

Proved by Croot [Cr03].
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
theorem erdos_46 (α : Type*) [Finite α] (c : ℕ → α) :
    ∃ S : Finset ℕ, S.Nonempty ∧
      (∀ n ∈ S, n ≥ 2) ∧
      (∃ color : α, ∀ n ∈ S, c n = color) ∧
      (∑ n ∈ S, (1 : ℚ) / (n : ℚ)) = 1 := by
  sorry

end Erdos46

lines: -      (∃ color : α, ∀ n ∈ S, c n = color) ∧
+        (∃ color : α, ∀ n ∈ S, c n = color) ∧
